retrieved_docs.title: Aplasia Cutis Congenita -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Aplasia cutis congenita (ACC) is a rare congenital absence of skin, involving the epidermis, dermis, and, occasionally, subcutaneous tissue. The condition is multifactorial, with proposed mechanisms including disruption of embryonic skin development from vascular compromise, amniotic band sequence, teratogens, trauma, or genetic mutations. Risk factors encompass intrauterine infections, maternal drug exposure, and chromosomal anomalies. The pathophysiology involves defective morphogenesis or destruction of previously formed skin during the 1st trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: Ichthyosis Fetalis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications that are commonly seen in the neonatal period include fulminant sepsis, respiratory failure, ectropion, eclabium, transepidermal water loss (TEWL), electrolyte imbalances, contractures, constricting skin bands leading to limb and or digital ischemic necrosis. Rajpopat et al. reported recurrent skin infections in early childhood and plantar changes leading to delay in walking due to palmoplantar keratoderma. Both heat and cold intolerance have been reported. Pruritis affected almost half of the survivors.

retrieved_docs.title: Aplasia Cutis Congenita -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most widely accepted pathophysiologic model describes tension that disrupts proper skin approximation during fetal development. Contributing exogenous factors may include ingested teratogens, fetal or placental ischemia, intrauterine infections, and NTDs. Recent investigations have identified dominant-negative mutations in the KCTD1 and KCTD15 genes as causes of ACC through functional impairment of KCTD1/KCTD15 complexes in cranial neural crest cells. This impairment disrupts the formation of normal midline cranial sutures and the overlying skin. [16]

retrieved_docs.content: Tibial deformities (Anterior bowing or "saber shins" and localized demineralization of the proximal medial tibia [Wimberger sign])

retrieved_docs.title: Amniotic Band Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital varicella syndrome: Associated with maternal infection and limb defects present in a dermatomal distribution, which is often symmetric

retrieved_docs.title: Phocomelia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Children with phocomelia have a high stillbirth rate compared to the general population. Furthermore, children with severe limb hypoplasia may or will face problems with activities of daily life and mobility. For this reason, health care teams must be integrated to provide for more than just the medical challenges that present with phocomelia.

retrieved_docs.title: Aplasia Cutis Congenita -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation of ACC begins with a detailed personal, obstetric, and family history, followed by a comprehensive physical examination. Special attention should be given to identifying associated anomalies or malformation syndromes, particularly limb and digit deformities. In the neonatal period, additional tests may include placental examination and viral testing for herpes simplex virus and varicella-zoster virus.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital and Maternal Syphilis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Condyloma lata

retrieved_docs.title: Amniotic Band Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Class 1: No signs of constriction Class 2a: No or mild lymphedema Class 2b: Severe lymphedema Class 3a: Abnormal distal Doppler studies Class 3b: No vascular flow to the extremity Class 4: Bowing or fracture of long bones Class 5: Intrauterine amputation

retrieved_docs.title: Aplasia Cutis Congenita -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: ACC lacks a single underlying cause and represents a visible manifestation of abnormal intrauterine skin development. The condition likely arises from diverse causes. Proposed etiologies include genetic factors, placental infarcts, trauma, teratogens such as methimazole, intrauterine infections, and neural tube defects (NTDs). [7] No specific genetic target had been identified until recently. However, new evidence has implicated the BMS1 gene as a possible contributor. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Conjoined Twins -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The intrauterine demise of the twins is likely given the complicated nature of the pregnancy. Postnatally, complications related to the complex anatomy of the twins may lead to death. If surgery is an option, complications related to separation include organ failure, skin defects, surgical infection, bleeding, injury to internal organs and/or vasculature, and failure to complete the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital and Maternal Syphilis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Perioral fissures or scarring

retrieved_docs.title: Aplasia Cutis Congenita -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: ACC is a heterogeneous group of disorders characterized by the congenital absence of skin in localized or extensive areas at birth. Most minor lesions resolve within the first few months of life, leaving an atrophic scar or, less commonly, a hypertrophic scar. In such cases, daily cleansing and application of petrolatum or a topical antibiotic ointment until complete healing is generally sufficient.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teratology TORCH -- Clinical Significance -- Other:
retrieved_docs.content: Syphilis infects 5.6 million people per year. [11] Pregnancies where the woman contracts syphilis and is not treated result in stillbirth 25% of the time, neonatal death 14% of the time and in 41% of cases the infant is born with congenital syphilis. Congenital syphilis can result in neurodevelopmental deficits, musculoskeletal deformities as well as renal and gastrointestinal issues. The classical Hutchinson triad described in infants with congenital syphilis consists of Hutchinson teeth (small, peg-shaped, notched upper incisors), interstitial keratitis and sensorineural hearing loss. There is only a 20% chance that an untreated affected mother will have a healthy infant. The longer the fetus is exposed to T. pallidum in utero the bleaker the outcome. Pregnant women can be treated with benzathine penicillin G to diminish the bacterial load and decrease the likelihood of complications during pregnancy. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Amniotic Band Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: However, the efficacy of this intervention is unknown, as there are no established criteria for candidate selection and a lack of clinical studies. [17] The primary risk associated with fetoscopic band release is preterm premature rupture of membranes, which affects 51% of cases in a recent systematic review. This results in a compounded risk of preterm delivery of over 70%. More than 75% of neonates in this study had preserved function of the affected limb; however, in 21.2% of cases, the limb required amputation postnatally to allow for prostheses. [18] Additional risks include fetal injury, intrauterine bleeding, and infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Aplasia Cutis Congenita -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Preventive care includes early dermatologic evaluation, infection surveillance, and timely surgical intervention when indicated. Parents should be offered genetic counseling if a strong family history of ACC is elicited or their child presents with ACC and a malformation syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 777
retrieved_docs.content: and uterine scars from previous surgeries, including previous cesarean sections, are specific indications that belong to the larger group of maternal‑care indications triggered by known or suspected pelvic‑organ abnormalities. These indications guide clinical decision‑making throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery, and they are consistently represented across Dutch and Arabic terminology, ensuring unified understanding and management of maternal health risks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2289
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormality of organs AND/OR soft tissues of pelvis affecting pregnancy is a broad category of conditions that can impact gestation, and within this category the specific condition Uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy represents a uterine scar that develops after a surgical procedure on the uterus (عملية في الرحم). The uterine scar is therefore classified as a particular pelvic organ/soft‑tissue abnormality that influences pregnancy outcomes. In clinical practice, the presence of such a uterine scar is taken into account when interpreting Pregnancy observable, as it may affect the assessment of fetal well‑being and maternal health. Recognizing that the scar occurs subsequent to عملية في الرحم helps clinicians anticipate potential complications and tailor prenatal monitoring accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN” is clinically associated with “ولادة قبل الأوان” (preterm birth). This relationship suggests that when a caesarean section scar cannot be classified, there may be an increased likelihood of delivering before term, highlighting the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management in patients with indeterminate scar types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2254
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy is classified as a specific abnormality of the organs and/or soft tissues of the pelvis that impacts pregnancy, representing a subset of pelvic organ and soft‑tissue abnormalities that can compromise gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified conditions related to pregnancy comprise a range of distinct gestational disorders. This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2061
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormality of organs AND/OR soft tissues of pelvis affecting pregnancy is a broad category of pelvic conditions that can compromise gestation, and within this category the specific entity Uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy represents a distinct abnormality. A uterine scar of this type arises as a consequence of a prior uterine surgery (عملية في الرحم) and therefore occurs after such operative procedures. Recognizing that a uterine scar is both a subset of pelvic organ/soft‑tissue abnormalities affecting pregnancy and a sequela of uterine surgery is essential for assessing obstetric risk, planning prenatal care, and managing potential complications related to scarred uterine tissue during pregnancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a category that includes several distinct conditions. It is classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery). Within this category are the specific complications zwangerschap; vermoeidheid (pregnancy‑related fatigue) and طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال (multiform rash of pregnancy). These entities represent various adverse events that can occur during gestation, underscoring the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and management of substance use, fatigue, and dermatologic manifestations to optimize maternal and fetal health.
